Well wrong, because it's completely fucking arbitrary - to the point it can even change between two way of calling the same object.
Washing machine, for example can be called "machine à laver" (female) or "lave-linge" (male).
On the plus side it sometime help differentiate between words that are written or sound the same.
"Une vis" (female) is a screw. "Un vice" (male) is the same as the english vice - both are pronounced the same.
"Le mousse" (male) is a low-rank mariner. "La mousse" (female) is moss.
And you will sound like an absolute psychopath if you get it wrong (unless you are clearly a foreigner in which case it's just cute).

We got an actual issue about it recently, with Covid.
Lots of disease are male, "Le Sida" (aids), "Le Cancer", "Le Rhume" (Cold), "Un virus", etc - despite disease itself ("La Maladie") being female.
So most people just rolled with "Le Covid".

>ALL of your power needs, forever.
>to solve energy FOREVER
Solar panels degrade with time. Sunlight slowly damages the panel materials. Heat causes microscopic cracks in the silicon. Even with "good panels" that you pay more for (which will take longer to get to your "ROI"), you can see them decline at around 0.3-0.8% per year. Meaning that over a long period of time. Paradoxically, the more sun you get the faster they can decline in efficiency.

Work out the total energy costs to make those panels. The energy required to refine the silver. The energy required to make the plastic. To refine the silicon. All of the fuel required for global logistics, to get it all to a place like China, and across China to assemble it. The cost to ship it thousands of miles from China to you. And the cost of fuel to haul all the fuel. Be it in an oil tanker or the vehicles that need to fill up the stations that the trucks use to move all this shit around on the road.

It turns out that once you factor that into the equation these panels are all a net negative. To compare, pick something like a diesel generator. Work out the energy costs to make that. Then work out the costs to use it to power your home in diesel. You'll find that it uses less energy, less hydrocarbons. Even it's "ROI" is faster. But that seems "dirty" to retards because they can see the byproduct at the end of the energy generation process, even though the whole process uses less emissions.

Most people should look into something like their own biogass system. Where you are feeding in things like kitchen scraps or weeds from the garden into a system that can get you your own methane. Use this to heat your home or cook or even generate electricity.

>oy vey that's dirty goyim
Natural gas is the cleanest of all fossil fuels, and the energy efficiency (to do this locally) is the best compared to the alternatives (like oil/coal) and certainly better than the lunatics who insist on "green" things like solar.

comment:
>>530516569
Cabling
Mounting
Conversion
Space
Battery (actually most expensive part)
Controllers/Safety
Building 4-8 times of current need to compensate for future higher demands and to compensate for degradation over time and to have enough to fill up batteries for night, cloudy/rainy days and winter time.

Let us say your entire home needs about 1kW per hour a day.
In an ideal 50/50 split of getting nothing and getting the maximum you can get you would need to generate realistically between 2.1 and 2.3 kWh.
1000kWh for your actual consumption and 1.1-1.3 kWh for the battery for night time as you need to account for 10-30% back and fourth conversion loss and other stuff.
Now come the issues.
Imagine you want to drive a power tool or any other additional thing that has a high draw.
Now you loose on you night time saving.
So you need to upscale even further.
But what about cloudy days or winter in general.
If you still get 50% output, you would need to upscale by a factor of 2, if you only get 25%, the factor rises to 4.
But now it is 10 years down the line and your panels degraded, so you get even less.
So you need to upscale for that as well.

The panels are less than half the actual cost.

>>530522187
By getting the jump and enlisting yourself before the draft.

I used to be that guy. No job, no clue, just stuck doomscrolling and wondering why nothing was working out. Life felt like a co-op game where I forgot to pick up a second controller. Then I tripped over something kind of wild the army's got this #QuickShip thing where you can pull in $40k just for signing and showing up. Not even joking.


I'm not trying to LARP as a drill sergeant or something. I'm just saying: if you're sitting around wondering how the hell to get ahead, this is one of those options that somehow exists but no one talks about unless your uncle brings it up at Thanksgiving. Except now they're throwing real money at people who are willing to step up.


I know what you're probably thinking. Military=war, scary stuff, not for me. I thought the same. Full-blown tech nerd over here, never touched a weight, social skills at 15%, maxed out in sarcasm and caffeine dependency. But the truth? Most recruits never see combat. You're more likely to end up learning stuff, getting in shape, and figuring your life out than you are running into anything risky.

It's more "life reboot" than battlefield. You build structure, get confidence, and yeah people actually treat you with more respect. You stop feeling like a background NPC energy and start showing up like a main character.


And the kicker? After your time's up, you've got $40k+, free education, benefits, the works. Start a business, buy a house, whatever. It's like real-life NG+ with unlockable perks that don't expire.


Not saying it's for everyone, but if you're even a little tired of sitting on the bench while life keeps loading might be worth looking at. No hype, no pressure, just putting it out there like someone should've done for me.

If you're curious, there's a site I found that breaks down how #QuickShip and bonuses work. Look it up, do your own digging. Just maybe don't dismiss it like I almost did.
